home.title: IAI Foundation - Intent OS / Universal Intent Infrastructure
home.description: IAI Foundation is building the trust layer that turns human and business intent into real-world outcomes with AI, workflows, payments, devices, approvals, and proof.
home.ogTitle: IAI Foundation - Intent OS / Universal Intent Infrastructure
home.ogDescription: A trust layer for turning intent into real-world outcomes across AI, workflows, payments, devices, approvals, and proof.
home.twitterTitle: IAI Foundation - Intent OS / Universal Intent Infrastructure
home.twitterDescription: Public foundation site for Intent OS, the trust layer between intent and real-world outcomes.
home.ogImageAlt: IAI Foundation Intent OS visual identity
home.lead: IAI Foundation is building the trust layer that coordinates AI, workflows, payments, devices, approvals, and proof in one system.

thesis.label: Thesis
thesis.title: The world has tools. It still lacks the execution layer.
paragraphs: We already have capable AI systems, durable workflows, connected devices, digital payments, and programmable software. What is still missing is the layer that can coordinate them under clear authority, policy, timing, and proof.

The world does not need another disconnected app. What is missing is a shared execution layer that can coordinate people, organizations, AI agents, workflows, money rails, connected devices, robots, approvals, evidence, and accountability.
IAI Foundation exists to define and build that layer.

whatIs.label: What Intent OS Is
whatIs.title: A trustable runtime between intent and outcome.
paragraphs: Intent OS is a system that converts a user or organization's intent into a real-world outcome with policy, orchestration, approvals, payments, device actions, and proof.
A user should be able to say: "I want this to happen, safely, under the right conditions, with the right authority, at the right time, with clear proof."
And the system should transform that request into a validated intent, an executable plan, a governed run, a recorded outcome, and an auditable proof trail.

cards.title: 1. Identity
cards.text: People, teams, agents, devices, and organizations.

cards.title: 2. Wallet
cards.text: Value, limits, rails, and delegated authority.

cards.title: 3. Intent
cards.text: Goals expressed in natural language or structured templates.

cards.title: 4. Orchestration
cards.text: Plans, workflows, approvals, retries, and exceptions.

cards.title: 5. Device
cards.text: Connected systems, gateways, sensors, and robot endpoints.

cards.title: 6. Proof
cards.text: Receipts, logs, signatures, telemetry, and evidence.

cards.title: 7. Policy
cards.text: Rules for what is allowed, when, by whom, and under what conditions.

cards.title: Business Operations Wallet
cards.text: Payable approvals, vendor release conditions, service completion proof, escalation rules, policy-based disbursement, and full audit trail for SMEs and operational teams.

cards.title: Personal Action Wallet
cards.text: Recurring obligation automation, subscription and service control, delegated AI tasks with guardrails, home and access actions, and proof of completion for individuals.

cards.title: Autonomous Ops Layer
cards.text: Procurement flows, facilities actions, field service orchestration, policy-controlled device workflows, and machine-triggered actions with human checkpoints for advanced teams.

cards.title: Vendor Release
cards.text: Release payment only when verified conditions are met, with policy-gated approvals and proof of delivery.

cards.title: AI + Human Approval
cards.text: Coordinate AI work, human approval checkpoints, and final settlement in a single governed loop.

cards.title: Governed Device Action
cards.text: Trigger facility or device actions under policy constraints with proof collection at every step.

proof.label: Trust & Proof
proof.title: Proof before trust claims.
paragraphs: Trust is not a claim. It is a verifiable chain of evidence. Every action in Intent OS produces a proof bundle: receipts, logs, signatures, telemetry, and human confirmations.
Without proof, automation is just speed without accountability. With proof, every outcome can be audited, disputed, reconciled, and trusted.
Proof is not an afterthought. It is a first-class output of every run, every payment, every device command, and every approval decision.

principles.label: Principles
principles.title: Built for authority, accountability, and restraint.
items: Money is a rail, not the brand
Devices must be governed, not merely connected
Every delegated action must be attributable
Default design must reduce blast radius
Logs are part of the product, not a side effect

roadmap.label: Roadmap
roadmap.title: What we are building and when.

items.title: 0 to 30 days
items.text: Launch the public website. Finalize category language. Publish architecture and principles. Create builder, partner, and pilot intake.

items.title: 30 to 90 days
items.text: Design control-plane schemas. Lock identity, workspace, intent, approval, and proof models. Publish public documentation v1.

items.title: 3 to 6 months
items.text: Build control-plane MVP. Build policy and runtime MVP. Support manual approvals and proof bundles.

items.title: 6 to 12 months
items.text: Add payment abstraction. Add vendor release and service completion use cases. Begin the first pilot with tight scope.

items.title: 12 to 24 months
items.text: Add device gateway abstraction. Expand to autonomous procurement and facilities pilots. Integrate Matter-compatible devices.

builders.label: Builders
builders.title: For builders of the next operational layer.
paragraphs: We are interested in developers, operators, researchers, infrastructure partners, and organizations exploring the future of trustable execution across software and the physical world.

This system is not another AI chat app, crypto wallet, IoT dashboard, or generic automation builder. It is a trustable runtime that converts intent into outcome with policy, orchestration, payments, device actions, human approvals, and proof in one loop.

contact.label: Contact
contact.title: Help define the next layer of operations.
contact.text: Pilot, builder, and partner conversations are open now.

languageSeoStandard.title: Language and Multilingual SEO Standard - IAI Foundation Docs
languageSeoStandard.description: Operational standard for page language, locale-specific SEO, Vietnamese metadata rules, and multilingual release gates.
languageSeoStandard.ogTitle: Language and Multilingual SEO Standard - IAI Foundation Docs
languageSeoStandard.ogDescription: Read the locked standard for page language, per-locale metadata, Vietnamese SEO, and multilingual expansion governance.
languageSeoStandard.twitterTitle: Language and Multilingual SEO Standard - IAI Foundation Docs
languageSeoStandard.twitterDescription: Locked operating standard for bilingual content, per-locale metadata, and multilingual release quality.
languageSeoStandard.ogImageAlt: Language and multilingual SEO standard document cover
languageSeoStandard.lead: This standard locks how IAIFoundation.com handles page language, route parity, locale-specific metadata, and multilingual release quality.
languageSeoStandard.summary: Treat each locale as a first-class product surface with its own meaning, metadata, and QA gate.

sections.label: Locked Baseline
sections.title: Use one route key across locales.
paragraphs: English stays on the root route. Every non-default locale uses a locale prefix while keeping the same route key.
Canonical URLs always point to the apex host and each locale page self-canonicalizes to its own URL.
items: Route parity required for home, docs, partners, and future public routes

sections.label: Language Governance
sections.title: One page, one dominant language.
paragraphs: Navigation, headings, body copy, CTA labels, metadata, and structured data must all match the page locale.
The only exceptions are locked brand or product names and approved technical nouns that would lose meaning if translated.
items: Do not mix English metadata into Vietnamese pages
Do not ship mixed-language navigation or footer labels
Do not change locked terminology without an approved decision record

sections.label: Vietnamese SEO
sections.title: Vietnamese is its own search surface.
paragraphs: Vietnamese content targets Vietnam-based readers and search behavior. It is not a side effect of English localization.
Every Vietnamese page must carry its own title, meta description, Open Graph copy, Twitter copy, structured data language, and internal anchor language.
items: Use full Vietnamese diacritics everywhere in visible UI and metadata
Use vi_VN as the Open Graph locale
Do not reuse the English keyword cluster on Vietnamese pages
Do not index a Vietnamese page before its metadata and schema are localized

sections.label: Release Gates
sections.title: No multilingual work is complete without proof.
paragraphs: A locale or SEO task is only done when the localized copy, metadata, hreflang cluster, sitemap, schema, and live smoke checks all agree.
The release team must use the locale brief and the multilingual QA checklist before a new locale or updated localized page goes live.
items: Update localized HTML, sitemap, locale manifest, and switchers together
Keep users inside the same locale whenever an equivalent route exists
Attach QA evidence before claiming completion
Escalate any change to route model, default locale, or locked terminology

relatedLinks.label: Open the locale SEO brief template
Open the multilingual release QA checklist

localeSeoBriefTemplate.title: Locale SEO Brief Template - IAI Foundation Docs
localeSeoBriefTemplate.description: Standard brief template for defining search intent, metadata, route parity, and QA gates before publishing a new locale.
localeSeoBriefTemplate.ogTitle: Locale SEO Brief Template - IAI Foundation Docs
localeSeoBriefTemplate.ogDescription: Use this template to lock route scope, keyword intent, metadata, and QA requirements for every new locale.
localeSeoBriefTemplate.twitterTitle: Locale SEO Brief Template - IAI Foundation Docs
localeSeoBriefTemplate.twitterDescription: Required operating template for multilingual route planning, metadata, and SEO release control.
localeSeoBriefTemplate.ogImageAlt: Locale SEO brief template document cover
localeSeoBriefTemplate.lead: Every new locale should begin with one brief that locks audience, search intent, metadata, route scope, and QA expectations before publish.
localeSeoBriefTemplate.summary: Use one brief per locale so search intent and release scope are explicit before content work begins.

sections.label: Brief Identity
sections.title: Start with ownership and market definition.
paragraphs: A locale brief should capture the locale code, region, product surface, release target date, and named owner before any writing starts.
This ensures the team is aligning on a market, not just a translation request.

sections.label: Search Intent
sections.title: Plan keyword intent by route, not by page title alone.
paragraphs: Each route needs a primary keyword cluster, supporting semantic terms, audience definition, and expected CTA.
The brief should separate informational, navigational, documentation, and partnership intent clearly.

sections.label: Metadata Pack
sections.title: Lock the metadata before the page ships.
paragraphs: Every indexable route should leave the brief with a planned H1, title, meta description, canonical, alternate locale map, and schema language.
This avoids a last-minute scramble that leads to duplicated or weak metadata.
items: Alternate locale map and schema language

sections.label: QA Gates
sections.title: A locale does not publish without proof.
paragraphs: The brief should define the pre-publish checks for localized copy, metadata, hreflang, sitemap, locale manifest, and live verification.
Evidence links should point to preview URLs, screenshots, and release notes.
items: Approval fields for content, SEO, QA, and product

relatedLinks.label: Read the language and multilingual SEO standard
Open the multilingual release QA checklist

multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.title: Multilingual Release QA Checklist - IAI Foundation Docs
multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.description: Release checklist for multilingual pages covering language quality, metadata, hreflang, sitemap, menu behavior, and live smoke tests.
multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.ogTitle: Multilingual Release QA Checklist - IAI Foundation Docs
multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.ogDescription: Use this shared checklist to gate multilingual releases across dev, content, QA, SEO, and AI operations.
multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.twitterTitle: Multilingual Release QA Checklist - IAI Foundation Docs
multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.twitterDescription: Shared QA checklist for multilingual content, metadata, browser behavior, and live smoke validation.
multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.ogImageAlt: Multilingual release QA checklist document cover
multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.lead: This checklist creates one shared release gate for dev, content, QA, SEO, and AI agents whenever language, metadata, or locale routing changes.
multilingualReleaseQaChecklist.summary: Run the same release checklist across copy, metadata, routing, menu behavior, and live smoke validation.

sections.label: Preflight
sections.title: Lock scope before touching production.
paragraphs: The affected locales, routes, and release note must be explicit before the team starts validating the release candidate.
No multilingual release should proceed with hidden scope or unreviewed copy.
items: Affected locales and routes are explicit
No placeholder or unreviewed machine translation remains

sections.label: Language QA
sections.title: Check visible UI text first.
paragraphs: Navigation, CTA labels, footer text, form copy, and error states must all match the active locale.
Vietnamese pages must use full diacritics and locked terminology. English pages must read naturally in international US English.
items: Vietnamese diacritics and English grammar are correct

sections.label: SEO QA
sections.title: Validate the metadata pack on every route.
paragraphs: Each indexable page needs a locale-specific title, description, canonical, hreflang cluster, Open Graph copy, Twitter copy, and schema language.
The checklist should catch duplicate metadata, missing alternates, and weak internal link language before release.
items: Localized Open Graph, Twitter, and schema fields

sections.label: Browser & Menu QA
sections.title: Do not separate language QA from browser QA.
paragraphs: Header navigation and language switching are part of the multilingual experience, especially on smaller viewports.
The team should confirm the menu opens, closes, resizes back to desktop cleanly, and does not trap the user in the wrong locale.
items: Header menu works on desktop, tablet, and mobile
Menu closes on link click, outside click, resize, and Escape
Language switcher points to a true equivalent page
Live HTML and assets match the intended release

relatedLinks.label: Read the language and multilingual SEO standard
Open the locale SEO brief template
